mnemonics.title: ECB Modes
mnemonics.content: ECB: Each Block is Separate - No chaining between blocks, making patterns visible

mnemonics.title: CBC Chain
mnemonics.content: CBC: Chain Blocks Together - Each block depends on previous ciphertext block plus current plaintext

mnemonics.title: AES Rounds
mnemonics.content: AES: 10-12-14 - 128 uses 10, 192 uses 12, 256 uses 14 rounds (increasing with key size)

mnemonics.title: DES Key
mnemonics.content: DES: 56 bits (7 bytes × 8 bits) - 8 parity bits included in 64-bit key

acronyms.usage: Current standard symmetric encryption algorithm, selected by NIST in 2001
Legacy symmetric encryption standard (1977-1998), now insecure
DES applied three times for enhanced security, being phased out
Simplest block cipher mode, encrypts blocks independently (insecure)
Mode where each block is XORed with previous ciphertext block
Mode using incremental counter for encryption, allows parallel processing
Random value used to ensure ciphertext uniqueness in modes like CBC
Theoretically unbreakable encryption using random key of message length
Standards for padding and key formats in cryptography

visualTips.visualization: Imagine block cipher as encrypting entire pages at once (batch processing), while stream cipher is like encrypting each word as you speak it (real-time, continuous)
Think of CBC as dominoes - each block 'knocks over' the next, creating a chain of dependencies. If one block is corrupted, all following blocks are affected.
Think of a image of a blue sky with white clouds encrypted in ECB - the clouds (similar plaintext blocks) remain visible as patterns in the encrypted image.
Imagine two people needing a secret code (key) to communicate. They must first meet in person to agree on the code - that's the key distribution problem.

analogies.analogy: Like a physical lock that uses the same key to lock and unlock a door. Both sender and receiver need copies of the same key.
Like translating each word of a book separately without context - 'bank' could mean river bank or money bank, losing the relationship between words.
Like a relay race where each runner (block) must touch the previous runner (ciphertext) before passing the baton (encrypted block).
Like needing to tell someone the password to your safe, but to tell them you need to put it in another safe that also needs a password.
Like a continuous water stream being filtered - each drop (bit/byte) is processed individually and immediately as it flows through.
